title: The conversation belongs to him
summary: A patient's daughter brings a difficult question to the palliative care doctor before he sees her father. He reframes what is actually being decided — and gives her a single sentence to use.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: Dr Tran. Before you go in to see my father, I want to ask you something. He does not know how short the time is. He has been told the words — 'serious illness', 'limited time' — but I do not think he has understood. My family is divided. Half want to spell it out clearly. Half want to let him stay where he is.

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: Sit, Mrs Phuong. We have time. I have done this conversation many times. Let me say what I think, and you can disagree.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: Please.

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: The most important thing is not what we tell him. It is whether he is allowed to know what he already knows. There is a quiet thing that happens with very ill patients. They almost always know more than their family thinks. They feel the bed in a different way. They hear how visitors speak to them. They notice that conversations stop when they enter. The question is not 'should we tell him'. The question is 'are we allowing him to mention what he has already concluded'.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: You think he knows.

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: I do not know what he knows. I am saying that the working assumption — that he is in the dark and we are the keepers of the truth — is, in my experience, wrong more often than it is right. The truth is usually in the room already. The family is the part of the room that has not caught up.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: That is uncomfortable to hear.

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: It is supposed to be. I do not mean it as a criticism of your family. The family's response is loving. Protecting someone from a hard fact is, in most settings, a kindness. It is only in this specific setting that the kindness produces a particular loneliness. The patient cannot say what he knows because everyone is performing for him.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: So what do you recommend?

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: I do not recommend a single conversation. I recommend a single sentence, asked rather than told. 'Dad, is there anything you would like to talk about, or arrange, while you are still feeling clear?' That is all. Then you wait. If he wants to use the opening, he will. If he does not, you have not forced him.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: And if he wants to use it?

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: Then you let him talk. You do not interrupt. You do not correct his prognosis if he is being more pessimistic than the doctors. You do not reassure him out of the conversation. You listen, you note any practical things he wants — a person he wants to see, a possession he wants to give, a piece of music he wants at the end — and you act on them.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: And if he chooses not to use the opening?

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: Then you have given him the option, which is the gift. You have not committed an intrusion. The next person who has half an hour alone with him can ask the same sentence. Eventually, in almost every case, someone gets used.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: Half of my family will not be able to ask that sentence without crying.

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: Then they should not be the ones to ask it. Choose the family member who can ask without performance — without theatre, without trembling. Often it is not the closest. Sometimes it is a niece or a brother-in-law. Pick the steady one.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: I do not know if I am the steady one.

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: You came to ask me first. That suggests you might be.

body.speaker: Phuong
body.text: Is there a wrong way to do this?

body.speaker: Dr Tran
body.text: Yes. The wrong way is to demand that he confront his death in a form you have decided. The wrong way is to be in love with the moment of telling. The conversation belongs to him. We are providing the seats.

annotations.meaningVi: chia rẽ, không đồng thuận
đã kết luận (sau khi tự suy xét)
tiên lượng (y học)
sự xâm phạm, sự xâm nhập không mời
sự diễn xuất, màn diễn (nghĩa bóng: cư xử giả tạo)
vững vàng, điềm tĩnh, không bị cảm xúc chi phối
nói thẳng, giải thích rõ ràng không úp mở
giả định mặc định đang được áp dụng (dù chưa được kiểm chứng)
người nắm giữ sự thật, người kiểm soát thông tin
không được biết, bị giữ trong vô minh (idiom)
bắt kịp, theo kịp (một sự thật hoặc tình huống)
khó nghe, khó tiếp nhận (nói về sự thật gây bất an)
sự thật khó chịu, thực tế phũ phàng
trong hầu hết các bối cảnh, hoàn cảnh thông thường
bối cảnh cụ thể này (nhấn mạnh sự khác biệt của hoàn cảnh)
đặt câu hỏi thay vì áp đặt — cách tiếp cận tôn trọng quyền tự quyết
nắm lấy cơ hội được tạo ra để nói điều mình muốn nói

annotations.phrase: reassure him out of the conversation
annotations.meaningVi: trấn an đến mức khiến đối phương không còn dám tiếp tục nói
đã thực hiện hành vi xâm phạm (ngôn ngữ trang trọng, văn phong y đức)
không kịch tính, không cường điệu cảm xúc
không run rẩy, không để lộ cảm xúc ra ngoài
theo kinh nghiệm của tôi (hedging language — làm mềm một khẳng định)

annotations.phrase: wrong more often than it is right
annotations.meaningVi: sai nhiều hơn đúng (nhấn mạnh bằng đối lập cân đối)
một dạng cô đơn đặc thù, cô đơn rất riêng trong bối cảnh này
đối diện cái chết của chính mình (trực tiếp, không né tránh)
cuộc trò chuyện thuộc về anh ấy — người bệnh mới là chủ thể
trong hầu hết mọi trường hợp
có người bị lợi dụng

annotations.phrase: half an hour alone with him
annotations.meaningVi: nửa tiếng đồng hồ ở một mình với ông ấy

annotations.phrase: while you are still feeling clear
annotations.meaningVi: trong khi bạn vẫn còn cảm thấy minh mẫn

grammarNotes.title: Redirective 'The question is not X. The question is Y.'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong văn thoại trang trọng C1, người nói thường dùng cấu trúc 'The question is not X. The question is Y.' để chuyển hướng cuộc tranh luận khỏi câu hỏi sai sang câu hỏi đúng. Trong bài: 'The question is not "should we tell him". The question is "are we allowing him to mention what he has already concluded".' Cấu trúc này lịch sự nhưng có sức nặng — nó khẳng định rằng câu hỏi gốc đã được đặt sai.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: At C1, the structure 'The question is not X. The question is Y.' is used to redirect a debate from the wrong question to the right one. From the dialogue: 'The question is not "should we tell him". The question is "are we allowing him to mention what he has already concluded".' It is polite but firm — it asserts that the original question was misframed.

cloze.template:
Phuong: Dr Tran. Before you go in to see my father, I want to ask you something. He does not know how short the time is. He has been told the words — 'serious illness', '{{b1}} time' — but I do not think he has understood. My family is {{b2}}. Half want to spell it out clearly. Half want to let him stay where he is.
Dr Tran: Sit, Mrs Phuong. We have time. I have done this conversation many times. Let me say what I think, and you can disagree.
Phuong: Please.
Dr Tran: The most important thing is not what we tell him. It is whether he is allowed to know what he already knows. There is a quiet thing that happens with very ill {{b3}}. They almost always know more than their family thinks. They feel the bed in a different way. They hear how visitors speak to them. They notice that conversations stop when they enter. The question is not 'should we tell him'. The question is 'are we allowing him to mention what he has already {{b4}}'.
Phuong: You think he knows.
Dr Tran: I do not know what he knows. I am saying that the working {{b5}} — that he is in the dark and we are the keepers of the truth — is, in my experience, wrong more often than it is right. The truth is usually in the room already. The family is the part of the room that has not {{b6}} up.
Phuong: That is uncomfortable to hear.
Dr Tran: It is supposed to be. I do not mean it as a criticism of your family. The family's response is loving. Protecting someone from a hard fact is, in most settings, a kindness. It is only in this specific setting that the kindness produces a particular {{b7}}. The patient cannot say what he knows because everyone is {{b8}} for him.
Phuong: So what do you recommend?
Dr Tran: I do not recommend a single conversation. I recommend a single {{b9}}, asked rather than told. 'Dad, is there anything you would like to talk about, or arrange, while you are still feeling clear?' That is all. Then you wait. If he wants to use the opening, he will. If he does not, you have not forced him.
Phuong: And if he wants to use it?
Dr Tran: Then you let him talk. You do not interrupt. You do not correct his {{b10}} if he is being more pessimistic than the doctors. You do not reassure him out of the conversation. You listen, you note any practical things he wants — a person he wants to see, a possession he wants to give, a piece of music he wants at the end — and you act on them.
Phuong: And if he chooses not to use the opening?
Dr Tran: Then you have given him the option, which is the gift. You have not committed an {{b11}}. The next person who has half an hour alone with him can ask the same sentence. Eventually, in almost every case, someone gets used.
Phuong: Half of my family will not be able to ask that sentence without crying.
Dr Tran: Then they should not be the ones to ask it. Choose the family member who can ask without {{b12}} — without theatre, without trembling. Often it is not the closest. Sometimes it is a {{b13}} or a brother-in-law. Pick the {{b14}} one.
Phuong: I do not know if I am the steady one.
Dr Tran: You came to ask me first. That suggests you might be.
Phuong: Is there a wrong way to do this?
Dr Tran: Yes. The wrong way is to demand that he {{b15}} his death in a form you have decided. The wrong way is to be in love with the moment of telling. The conversation belongs to him. We are providing the {{b16}}.

blanks.explanation: He has been told the words 'serious illness, limited time' — but Phuong worries he has not understood.
Phuong's family is divided about whether to spell it out clearly.
Very ill patients — the doctor's clinical experience.
Has the family allowed him to mention what he has already concluded — privately, on his own?
The working assumption that the patient is in the dark and the family keeps the truth.
The family has not caught up to what the patient already knows.
Kindness in this specific setting produces a particular loneliness.
Everyone is performing for him — the loving cover that traps him in his own knowledge.
He recommends a single sentence — asked rather than told.
Do not correct his prognosis even if he is more pessimistic than the doctors.
Giving the option is a gift. Not an intrusion.
Choose someone who can ask without performance — without theatre or trembling.
Often the steady one is a niece, or a brother-in-law — not necessarily the closest.
Pick the steady one to ask.
The wrong way is to demand he confront his death in a form you have decided.
'The conversation belongs to him. We are providing the seats.'

translationVi:
Phương: Bác sĩ Trần. Trước khi anh vào gặp bố tôi, tôi muốn hỏi anh một điều. Bố tôi không biết thời gian còn lại ngắn đến mức nào. Bố đã được nghe các từ — 'bệnh nghiêm trọng', 'thời gian hạn chế' — nhưng tôi không nghĩ bố đã hiểu. Gia đình tôi chia rẽ. Một nửa muốn nói thẳng. Một nửa muốn để bố ở yên chỗ bố đang ở.
BS Trần: Mời chị ngồi, chị Phương. Mình có thời gian. Tôi đã có cuộc trò chuyện này nhiều lần. Để tôi nói tôi nghĩ gì, chị có thể không đồng ý.
Phương: Anh nói đi.
BS Trần: Điều quan trọng nhất không phải là chúng ta nói gì với bác. Mà là bác có được phép biết cái bác đã biết hay không. Có một điều lặng lẽ xảy ra với các bệnh nhân rất nặng. Hầu như họ luôn biết nhiều hơn gia đình nghĩ. Họ cảm cái giường khác đi. Họ nghe khách thăm nói với họ khác đi. Họ để ý các cuộc trò chuyện dừng lại khi họ bước vào. Câu hỏi không phải 'mình có nên nói với bác không'. Câu hỏi là 'mình có đang để cho bác nói ra điều bác đã tự kết luận không'.
Phương: Anh nghĩ bố tôi đã biết.
BS Trần: Tôi không biết bác biết gì. Tôi đang nói rằng giả định mặc định — rằng bác đang ở trong bóng tối còn chúng ta là người giữ sự thật — theo kinh nghiệm của tôi, sai nhiều hơn đúng. Sự thật thường đã ở trong phòng rồi. Gia đình là phần trong phòng chưa bắt kịp.
Phương: Nghe khó chịu thật.
BS Trần: Đúng là phải khó chịu. Tôi không có ý chê trách gia đình chị. Phản ứng của gia đình là yêu thương. Bảo vệ ai đó khỏi một sự thật khó, trong hầu hết các bối cảnh, là một sự tử tế. Chỉ trong bối cảnh cụ thể này, sự tử tế ấy tạo ra một dạng cô đơn đặc thù. Bệnh nhân không thể nói cái mình biết vì ai cũng đang diễn cho mình xem.
Phương: Vậy anh khuyên gì ạ?
BS Trần: Tôi không khuyên một cuộc trò chuyện. Tôi khuyên một câu duy nhất, hỏi thay vì nói. 'Bố, có gì bố muốn nói, hay sắp xếp, khi bố còn cảm thấy minh mẫn không?' Chỉ vậy thôi. Rồi chị đợi. Nếu bác muốn dùng cơ hội đó, bác sẽ dùng. Nếu không, chị cũng không ép.
Phương: Còn nếu bố muốn dùng?
BS Trần: Thì chị để bác nói. Đừng ngắt lời. Đừng sửa tiên lượng của bác nếu bác đang bi quan hơn các bác sĩ. Đừng trấn an để bác phải bước ra khỏi cuộc trò chuyện. Chị nghe, chị ghi lại những điều thực tế bác muốn — một người bác muốn gặp, một vật bác muốn cho ai, một bản nhạc bác muốn lúc cuối — và chị làm.
Phương: Còn nếu bố chọn không dùng cơ hội đó?
BS Trần: Thì chị đã trao bác lựa chọn, và đó là món quà. Chị không xâm phạm. Người tiếp theo có nửa tiếng một mình với bác có thể hỏi cùng một câu. Cuối cùng, hầu như mọi trường hợp, sẽ có ai đó được dùng.
Phương: Một nửa gia đình tôi không thể hỏi câu đó mà không khóc.
BS Trần: Vậy họ không nên là người hỏi. Hãy chọn người trong gia đình có thể hỏi mà không diễn — không sân khấu, không run rẩy. Thường đó không phải là người gần nhất. Đôi khi là một cháu gái hay một anh rể. Hãy chọn người vững.
Phương: Tôi không biết tôi có phải là người vững không.
BS Trần: Chị đến hỏi tôi trước. Điều đó cho thấy có thể chị là vậy.
Phương: Có cách làm sai không ạ?
BS Trần: Có. Cách sai là ép bác đối diện cái chết theo hình thức chị đã định. Cách sai là yêu chính cái khoảnh khắc nói ra. Cuộc trò chuyện thuộc về bác. Mình chỉ đang sắp ghế.

questions.prompt: Why has Phuong stopped Dr Tran before he goes in?
options: She is afraid of the doctor
Her family is divided — half want to spell out the prognosis clearly; half want to leave her father where he is
questions.explanation: 'My family is divided. Half want to spell it out clearly. Half want to let him stay where he is.'

questions.prompt: What is Dr Tran's central reframing of the question?
options: The question is whether to tell or not to tell
The question is not 'should we tell him' but 'are we allowing him to mention what he has already concluded'
The question is who tells him
The question is when to tell him
questions.explanation: 'The question is not "should we tell him". The question is "are we allowing him to mention what he has already concluded".'
questions.hint: Look at his first long turn.

questions.prompt: What does Dr Tran say very ill patients almost always do?
options: Know more than their family thinks — they feel the bed differently, hear how visitors speak, notice that conversations stop when they enter
questions.explanation: His description of the quiet thing that happens.
questions.hint: Look at his first long turn.

questions.prompt: What 'working assumption' does Dr Tran reject?
options: That patients are too sick to talk
That the patient is in the dark and the family keeps the truth — 'wrong more often than it is right'
questions.explanation: 'The working assumption… that he is in the dark and we are the keepers of the truth — is, in my experience, wrong more often than it is right.'
questions.hint: Look at his second long turn.

questions.prompt: Why does kindness produce 'a particular loneliness' in this setting?
options: Because patients are alone in hospital
Because everyone is performing for the patient, who cannot say what he knows
questions.explanation: 'The patient cannot say what he knows because everyone is performing for him.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn about kindness.

questions.prompt: What does Dr Tran recommend instead of a big conversation?
options: A single sentence — asked rather than told: 'Dad, is there anything you would like to talk about, or arrange, while you are still feeling clear?'
questions.hint: Look at his turn about the single sentence.

questions.prompt: What should the family do if the father wants to use the opening?
options: Let him talk; not interrupt; not correct his prognosis; not reassure him out of the conversation; note and act on practical wishes
questions.explanation: Detailed in his turn about what to do if he uses the opening.
questions.hint: Look at his list of behaviours.

questions.prompt: What should the family do if he does NOT use the opening?
options: Accept that giving the option is itself the gift — not an intrusion; the next person can ask the same sentence later
questions.explanation: 'You have given him the option, which is the gift. You have not committed an intrusion.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn about not using the opening.

questions.prompt: Who should ask the sentence, in Dr Tran's view?
options: Whoever can ask without performance — without theatre, without trembling. Often a niece or a brother-in-law; the steady one
questions.explanation: 'Choose the family member who can ask without performance.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn about who should ask.

questions.prompt: Why does Dr Tran think Phuong might be the steady one?
options: She came to ask him first
questions.explanation: 'You came to ask me first. That suggests you might be.'

questions.prompt: What does Dr Tran call the 'wrong way' to do this?
options: To demand that the patient confront his death in a form the family has decided; to be 'in love with the moment of telling'
questions.explanation: 'The wrong way is to demand that he confront his death in a form you have decided. The wrong way is to be in love with the moment of telling.'
questions.hint: Look at the end of the dialogue.

questions.prompt: What is the final image of the dialogue?
options: 'The conversation belongs to him. We are providing the seats.'

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closer to Dr Tran's central position?
options: Families should always 'tell' dying patients the truth bluntly.
Most very ill patients already know; the family's task is not to tell, but to provide an opening, with the steadiest person, and to let the patient choose to use it.
Families should never speak of death.
Doctors should manage all family conversations.
questions.explanation: His reframing and his single-sentence recommendation are the through-line.
questions.hint: Look at the reframed question and the recommended sentence.

questions.prompt: Which of these is Dr Tran NOT claiming?
options: That a single sentence, asked rather than told, can serve as an opening
That the steadiest family member should ask the sentence, even if not the closest
That families should never protect their loved ones from hard facts in any setting
That the patient's prognosis should not be corrected during such a conversation
questions.explanation: 'Protecting someone from a hard fact is, in most settings, a kindness.' He limits the critique to the dying setting.
questions.hint: Look at his nuanced comment about kindness.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the conversation?
options: Patient and exacting — Dr Tran takes Phuong's question seriously, reframes it without scolding, and gives her a concrete sentence to use
questions.explanation: He sets time aside, names the discomfort, and ends with a steady, practical close.
questions.hint: Look at his opening 'Sit, Mrs Phuong' and his final lines.

questions.prompt: What does Dr Tran say about the practical things the patient may want?
options: Listen and note them — a person he wants to see, a possession he wants to give, a piece of music he wants at the end — and act on them
questions.explanation: 'You listen, you note any practical things he wants… and you act on them.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn about what to do during the conversation.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Dr Tran says: 'The truth is usually in the room already. The family is the part of the room that has not caught up.' Think of a difficult truth in your own family — about an illness, a relationship, a plan. Who in the room has already understood, and who is still being protected? What would change if the protection were quietly withdrawn?
